splits.legalQuestion: Whether a multiemployer pension plan actuary may select actuarial assumptions for calculating withdrawal liability after the statutory measurement date (the end of the prior plan year), or whether all such assumptions must be fixed by that date.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether ERISA requires actuarial assumptions used to calculate employer withdrawal liability to be locked in by the end of the prior plan year or whether they can be selected afterward. This affects billions of dollars in potential obligations for thousands of employers participating in multiemployer pension plans.

positions.label: Assumptions must be fixed by measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that all actuarial assumptions must be established by the statutory measurement date (end of the prior plan year), providing employers greater predictability about potential withdrawal liability.

positions.label: Assumptions may be selected after measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that plan actuaries may select actuarial assumptions after the statutory measurement date, allowing use of more current data in calculating withdrawal liability.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: M & K Employee Solutions v. Trustees of the IAM National Pension Fund

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the term of supervised release is tolled (paused) when a supervisee absconds from supervision, thereby extending the period during which the person can be found to have violated the conditions of supervised release.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether federal courts may apply a judicially-created 'fugitive tolling' doctrine to supervised release, effectively extending the period of court-ordered supervision beyond the time specified in the judgment when a person absconds. The outcome affects thousands of individuals who abscond from supervised release each year and raises fundamental questions about judicial power to extend punishment without explicit statutory authorization.

positions.label: Tolling applies
positions.summary: Holds that the term of supervised release is tolled when a supervisee absconds, so the supervised release period does not run during the time the person is a fugitive.

positions.label: No tolling
positions.summary: Holds that supervised release is not tolled during absconding because no statute authorizes such tolling, and the term continues to run regardless of the supervisee's fugitive status.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 2244(b)(1)'s bar on relitigating previously raised claims in habeas proceedings applies to federal prisoners filing motions under Section 2255.
splits.description: Circuits are split 6-to-3 on whether federal prisoners face the categorical bar of § 2244(b)(1) — which prohibits raising any claim previously presented — when bringing successive collateral attacks under § 2255, or whether they need only satisfy § 2255(h)'s requirements of newly discovered evidence or a new rule of constitutional law. This affects the gatekeeping standards for thousands of federal prisoners seeking post-conviction relief.

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) applies to § 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that the categorical bar of § 2244(b)(1) applies to federal prisoners' successive § 2255 motions, meaning they are barred from relitigating any previously presented claim in addition to satisfying § 2255(h)'s requirements.

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) does not apply to § 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that § 2244(b)(1)'s categorical bar does not apply to federal prisoners' § 2255 motions, so they need only satisfy § 2255(h)'s own gatekeeping requirements for successive motions.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a candidate for federal office has Article III standing to challenge a state law that allows the counting of mail-in ballots received after Election Day.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether candidates have standing to bring pre-election challenges to voting procedures. The resolution determines whether candidates can challenge rules on mail-in voting, ballot counting deadlines, and other election administration practices before an election takes place, or whether such disputes must wait until after the election.

positions.label: Standing exists
positions.summary: Holds that candidates have Article III standing to challenge election rules pre-election because vote dilution or competitive injury from allegedly unlawful ballot-counting procedures constitutes a cognizable injury in fact.

positions.label: No standing
positions.summary: Holds that candidates lack Article III standing for pre-election challenges to ballot-counting rules because any alleged injury is too speculative before the election takes place.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the rule of lenity trumps deference to agency or Sentencing Commission interpretations in criminal sentencing contexts.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether lenity should prevail over Auer/Kisor deference when interpreting ambiguous Sentencing Guidelines or criminal regulations. At least three circuits hold lenity trumps deference, while the Third Circuit panel held deference trumps lenity. The split is especially significant after Loper Bright overruled Chevron deference.

positions.label: Lenity trumps deference
positions.summary: Holds that the rule of lenity takes priority over agency or Sentencing Commission deference when interpreting ambiguous criminal provisions, preventing agencies from expanding criminal liability without proper lawmaking.

positions.label: Deference trumps lenity
positions.summary: Holds that agency or Sentencing Commission deference prevails over the rule of lenity, allowing deference to anti-defendant interpretations of ambiguous Guidelines provisions.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the word 'and' in the First Step Act's safety-valve provision, 18 U.S.C. § 3553(f)(1), is conjunctive (requiring all three criminal-history criteria to disqualify a defendant) or disjunctive (any one criterion is disqualifying).
splits.description: Circuits are deeply split on whether defendants are disqualified from safety-valve relief only if they meet all three criminal-history criteria listed in § 3553(f)(1), or whether possessing any one of the criteria is disqualifying. This split means defendants with identical criminal histories may receive significantly different sentences depending on which circuit they are in.

positions.label: 'And' is conjunctive (all three required)
positions.summary: Holds that defendants are disqualified from safety-valve relief only if they meet all three criminal-history criteria in § 3553(f)(1), reading 'and' in its ordinary conjunctive sense.

positions.label: 'And' is disjunctive (any one disqualifies)
positions.summary: Holds that possessing any one of the three criminal-history criteria in § 3553(f)(1) is sufficient to disqualify a defendant from safety-valve relief, reading 'and' as 'or' in context.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Application Note 1 to U.S.S.G. § 4B1.2, which includes inchoate offenses like conspiracy in the definition of 'controlled substance offense,' is entitled to deference under Stinson/Kisor when the guideline text does not mention inchoate offenses.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether conspiracy and other inchoate offenses qualify as 'controlled substance offenses' for the career-offender enhancement, based on whether the Sentencing Commission's commentary (Application Note 1) can expand the guideline text to include such offenses. Some circuits defer to the commentary, while others hold the text is unambiguous and excludes inchoate offenses.

positions.label: Commentary is authoritative — inchoate offenses included
positions.summary: Holds that Application Note 1 is entitled to deference as the Commission's authoritative interpretation, and thus conspiracy and other inchoate offenses qualify as controlled substance offenses under § 4B1.2.

positions.label: Guideline text is unambiguous — inchoate offenses excluded
positions.summary: Holds that the text of § 4B1.2(b) unambiguously excludes inchoate offenses, and commentary cannot expand the definition beyond the guideline's plain language.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the First Step Act's non-retroactive amendment eliminating mandatory consecutive 'stacked' sentences under 18 U.S.C. § 924(c) may be considered as an extraordinary and compelling reason for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether district courts may consider the First Step Act's anti-stacking amendment to § 924(c) — which Congress made non-retroactive — as a basis for compassionate release. Some circuits hold that non-retroactive statutory changes cannot constitute extraordinary and compelling reasons, while others allow courts to consider them. The Sentencing Commission's 2023 policy statement at § 1B1.13(b)(6) attempted to authorize such consideration, deepening the conflict.

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes cannot support compassionate release
positions.summary: Holds that the First Step Act's anti-stacking amendment to § 924(c) cannot be an extraordinary and compelling reason for compassionate release because Congress chose not to make the amendment retroactive, and the Sentencing Commission lacks authority to override that congressional choice.

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes may be considered
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may consider non-retroactive changes in law, including the First Step Act's anti-stacking amendment, as one factor among others when evaluating compassionate release motions.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether post-petition, pre-conversion increases in the equity of a debtor's asset belong to the bankruptcy estate or to the debtor upon good-faith conversion from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7 under 11 U.S.C. § 348(f)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether appreciation in property value that occurs between the filing of a Chapter 13 petition and conversion to Chapter 7 belongs to the bankruptcy estate or to the debtor. The Ninth Circuit held such appreciation belongs to the estate, while other circuits have held it belongs to the debtor, creating a split on the interpretation of § 348(f)(1)(A).

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to estate
positions.summary: Holds that the plain language of § 348(f)(1)(A), coupled with § 541(a)'s broad definition of estate property, means that post-petition pre-conversion appreciation inures to the bankruptcy estate upon conversion.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to debtor
positions.summary: Holds that under § 348(f)(1)(A), post-petition pre-conversion appreciation belongs to the debtor, consistent with the statute's structure, purpose, and legislative history protecting good-faith converting debtors.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the civil or criminal appeal deadline applies to orders resolving Hyde Amendment motions for attorney's fees in criminal cases.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case (subject to the 30-day civil deadline) or part of the criminal case itself (subject to the 14-day criminal deadline). The Seventh Circuit joined the majority view holding the civil deadline applies.

positions.label: Civil deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case, so the 30-day civil appeal deadline under Fed. R. App. P. 4(a) governs.

positions.label: Criminal deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment motion is part of the criminal case itself, so the 14-day criminal appeal deadline under Fed. R. App. P. 4(b) governs.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether district courts may expressly punish defendants for violations of supervised release at revocation sentencing, or whether revocation sentences must be based solely on breach of trust and the need for further supervision.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether revocation of supervised release may be punitive. The Sixth Circuit's precedent in United States v. Lewis allows courts to punish defendants for supervised release violations, while other circuits hold that revocation sentences should focus on the breach of trust with the court and the need to protect the public, not punishment for the underlying violation. This affects hundreds of individuals facing revocations each year.

positions.label: Punishment is permissible
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may expressly impose revocation sentences as punishment for violations of supervised release conditions.

positions.label: Punishment is not the purpose
positions.summary: Holds that revocation of supervised release should be based on the breach of the court's trust and the need to protect the public, not as punishment for the underlying violation.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Aviation Administration Authorization Act of 1994 (FAAAA) preempts state-law negligence claims against freight brokers who arrange transportation of property by motor carriers.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether the FAAAA's broad preemption clause, which bars states from enacting or enforcing laws 'related to a price, route, or service of any... broker,' preempts common-law negligence claims arising from a broker's selection of a motor carrier. This affects liability standards for the entire freight brokerage industry.

positions.label: FAAAA preempts broker negligence claims
positions.summary: Holds that state-law negligence claims against freight brokers are preempted because they relate to the broker's 'service' of arranging transportation, falling within the FAAAA's preemption clause.

circuits.name: Ninth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Miller v. C.H. Robinson Worldwide, Inc.

positions.label: FAAAA does not preempt broker negligence claims
positions.summary: Holds that common-law negligence claims against freight brokers are not preempted because they do not relate to the broker's prices, routes, or services within the meaning of the FAAAA.

circuits.name: Eleventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II, LLC

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II, LLC

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a district court may consider factors related to potential errors in a defendant's conviction or sentence — claims that might also be raised under § 2255 — as part of the 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' analysis for a sentence reduction under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether compassionate release can serve as a vehicle for addressing alleged legal errors in a conviction or sentence that would ordinarily be raised in a habeas petition. Some circuits permit courts to consider such claims as one factor in the compassionate release analysis, while others hold that § 3582(c)(1)(A) cannot be used to circumvent the requirements of § 2255.

positions.label: May consider conviction-related errors
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may consider potential legal errors in a defendant's conviction or sentence as part of the extraordinary and compelling reasons analysis under § 3582(c)(1)(A).

positions.label: Cannot use compassionate release for conviction errors
positions.summary: Holds that § 3582(c)(1)(A) cannot be used to address claims of legal error that properly belong in a § 2255 motion, and such claims do not constitute extraordinary and compelling reasons.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the former U.S.S.G. § 4C1.1(a)(10) disqualifies a defendant from the zero-point-offender reduction only if the defendant both received an aggravating role adjustment under § 3B1.1 and engaged in a continuing criminal enterprise, or if either condition alone is disqualifying.
splits.description: Circuits disagreed on whether the two disqualifying conditions in former § 4C1.1(a)(10) — receiving a § 3B1.1 role adjustment and engaging in a continuing criminal enterprise — must both be present to disqualify a defendant, or if either alone suffices. Every circuit to address the issue held that either condition alone disqualifies, and the Commission subsequently amended the guideline to clarify this. The Eighth Circuit joined this consensus.

positions.label: Either condition alone disqualifies
positions.summary: Holds that a § 3B1.1 aggravating role enhancement is sufficient by itself to disqualify a defendant from the § 4C1.1(a) two-level reduction, even without engagement in a continuing criminal enterprise.

circuits.name: Eighth Circuit
circuits.caseName: United States v. Medina de la Cruz

positions.label: Both conditions required
positions.summary: Holds that the plain language of § 4C1.1(a)(10) requires both a § 3B1.1 role adjustment and engagement in a continuing criminal enterprise to disqualify a defendant from the reduction.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether an order denying a motion to seal court filings referencing private medical treatments and diagnoses is immediately appealable under the collateral order doctrine.
splits.description: The D.C. Circuit held that denial of a motion to seal medical information is immediately appealable because maintaining medical privacy is a 'high order' value effectively unreviewable after final judgment. This potentially creates tension with circuits that apply a narrower view of the collateral order doctrine for sealing orders.

positions.label: Immediately appealable
positions.summary: Holds that orders denying motions to seal medical information satisfy the collateral order doctrine because medical privacy is a value of high order that would be irreparably lost if review is delayed until final judgment.

positions.label: Not immediately appealable
positions.summary: Holds that orders denying motions to seal are not immediately appealable under the collateral order doctrine because adequate alternative means exist to protect the interests at stake.
